documents.name: Pixel_AI notes.md
documents.excerpt:
# Pixel_AI brainstorming

## The idea of Pixel-based

- Each spot in a tissue could be considered as mixture of tissue
 > archetypes (for example tumor infiltration contains xx% of tumor
 > and xx% of immune cells). But we don't know the archetypes yet.

- Finding the archetypes means finding the niches in tissue - this
 > will be the new framework of describing and analyzing a tissue in
 > spatia

identity.research_question: Deep learning computational pipeline utilizing a Variational Autoencoder (VAE) and Vector Quantized VAE (VQ-VAE) model (Tissue Microenvironment Encoder - TMEncoder) to extract explainable latent embeddings directly from multi-channel multiplexed images (multiplex CycIF) without prior cell segmentati
identity.project_summary: Deep learning computational pipeline utilizing a Variational Autoencoder (VAE) and Vector Quantized VAE (VQ-VAE) model (Tissue Microenvironment Encoder - TMEncoder) to extract explainable latent embeddings directly from multi-channel multiplexed images (multiplex CycIF) without prior cell segmentation. Reduces 30+ channel dimensions into under 10 dimensions representing tissue archetypes. Applied to unsupervised image profiling (spatial motifs and recurrent cellular neighborhood mapping) and automatic ROI selection for spatial transcriptomics (GeoMx/Xenium) using latent space embeddings, downstream classifiers, and Grad-CAM visualization. Merged as a core component of Project 12: SPACE. **Description:** Deep learning computational pipeline utilizing a Variational Autoencoder (VAE) and Vector Quantized VAE (VQ-VAE) model (Tissue Microenvironment Encoder - TMEncoder) to extract explainable latent embeddings directly from multi-channel multiplexed images (multiplex CycIF) without prior cell segmentation. Reduces 30+ channel dimensions into under 10 dimensions representing tissue archetypes. Applied to unsupervised image profiling (spatial motifs and recurrent cellular neighborhood mapping) and automatic ROI selection for spatial transcriptomics (GeoMx/Xenium) using latent space embeddings, downstream classifiers, and Grad-CAM visualization. Merged as a core component of Project 12: SPACE.
identity.responsible: Ziqi Kang (Matias Aiskovich, Olavi Goussev, Angela Szabo, Fernando Perez)

# A deep learning framework for quantitative spatial analysis of the tumor microenvironment

**Ziqi Kang^1^, Matias Aiskovich^1^, Fernando Perez^1^, Angela Szabo^1^,
Lilian van Wagensveld^5,\ 6,\ 7^, Hugo Horlings^7^,** **Esa
Pitkänen^2,\ 3,\ 4^, Anniina Färkkilä^1,\ 2,\ 3^**

^1^ Research Program in Systems Oncology, University of Helsinki,
Finland

^2^ Institute for Molecular Medicine Finland, Helsinki Institute of Life
Sciences, University of Helsinki, Finland

^3^ iCAN Digital Precision Cancer Medicine Flagship, Finland

^4^ Applied Tumor Genomics Research Program, University of Helsinki,
Finland

^5^ Department of Research and Development, Netherlands Comprehensive
Cancer Organization (IKNL), 3511 DT Utrecht, the Netherlands

^6^ GROW, School for Oncology and Developmental Biology, Maastricht
University, 6229 ER Maastricht, the Netherlands

^7^ Division of Molecular Pathology, The Netherlands Cancer Institute,
1066 CX Amsterdam, The Netherlands

Current multiplexed immunofluorescence (mIF) image analysis relies on
quantitative description of single-cell objects, however, cell
segmentation is becoming a bottleneck for tissue architecture
description since it fails in dense regions and causes loss of
information in extracellular areas. There is a need to define novel
representations of complex tissue architecture, one possible solution is

vector_chunks.text:
ls in dense regions and causes loss of
information in extracellular areas. There is a need to define novel
representations of complex tissue architecture, one possible solution is
to extract biologically meaningful multi-scale features directly from
images without cell segmentation.

We present TMEncoder, a deep learning framework based on the variational
autoencoder (VAE) capable of extracting features of tumor
microenvironments based on multiplex-stained tumor tissue images without
expert-annotated labels. Based on the scale of input datasets, TMEncoder
could unsupervised learn corresponding features from local tissue
archetypes to tumor subtypes. TMEncoder incorporates multiple advanced
techniques including patch generation, intermediate fusion strategy, and
a novel pipeline for quantitative analysis of tumor microenvironment
elements. Therefore, our framework can process multi-channel images.

We applied TMEncoder to an in-house 33-plex tissue microarray (TMA)
dataset collected from a cohort of 241 high-grade serous ovarian cancer
(HGSOC) patients and successfully recovered biological features related
to tumor molecular subtypes. Then, we are planning to expand the
analysis to two external datasets of human cancer tissue images to
discover the tumor microenvironment features: a 56-plex TMA dataset
including 140 images from 35 advanced-stage colorectal cancer (CRC)

l datasets of human cancer tissue images to
discover the tumor microenvironment features: a 56-plex TMA dataset
including 140 images from 35 advanced-stage colorectal cancer (CRC)
patients; and a 35-plex imaging mass cytometry (IMC) dataset from 416
lung adenocarcinoma patients.

In summary, the novel image analysis strategy enabled by TMEncoder shows
promise to strengthen the understanding of tumor biology and re-visit
the hidden information behind multiplexed images. Combining such
information with classical single-cell-based analysis leads to an
improved understanding of the tumor microenvironment and novel precision
therapies.

## **2 SUMMARY OF THE PROJECT (PUBLIC ABSTRACT)**

*CSC will use this abstract (with some other basic information, such as
the size of the project) when publishing the selected proposals. **Max
1000 characters**.*

We aim to study the tumor microenvironment (TME) of high-grade serous
ovarian cancer to understand the basis of immune evasion and identify
cell-cell spatial biomarkers associated with clinical prognosis.

Using highly multiplexed imaging for single-cell spatial profiling, we
identified a distinct TME with evidence of increased spatial
immunosurveillance and distinct prognostication in ovarian cancer. In
this ERC Starting grant- funded project, we are employing highplex
microscopy and deep learning approach to identify characteristics in the
TME associated with the clinical outcomes in a large dataset of 300
patients participating in the Oncosys-OVA clinical trial (NCT06117384)
at the Helsinki university Hospital. Our current approach overcomes the
limitations of cell segmentation, as it uses patches of
highly-multiplexed images as input to identify common structures
associated with the clinico-molecular characteristics of the tumours.

## **3.1 KEY SCIENTIFIC/SOCIETAL/TECHNOLOGICAL CONTRIBUTION OF THE PROPOSAL (MAX 2000 CHARACTERS)**

## **3.1 KEY SCIENTIFIC/SOCIETAL/TECHNOLOGICAL CONTRIBUTION OF THE PROPOSAL (MAX 2000 CHARACTERS)**

[Outline the scientific/societal/technological importance of your
project, how high-performance computing (HPC) will help you achieve your
goals and what the major expected outcomes are.]{.mark}

Every year 240 000 women are diagnosed, and 150 000 women die from
ovarian cancer (OC) worldwide. High-grade serous OC (HGSC) is the most
common and most lethal subtype of ovarian cancer. Around 50% of HGSC
patients are treated with DNA damaging chemotherapies in the neoadjuvant
setting. However, the majority of these patients develop
chemoresistance, and new treatments are urgently needed for HGSC. It has
been shown that some mutations of HGSC can shape its tumor
microenvironment in a way that patients could tentatively get benefits
from immunotherapy. Ovarian cancer response to immunotherapy is still
limited, and a better understanding of the tumor microenvironment (TME)
is required.

We are developing tools and a novel pipeline for the analysis of high
plex imaging combined with spatial transcriptomics of the TME. Our tools
are based on the use of GPUs for the deep learning analysis of a large
volume of imaging and transcriptomics data. The results of this project
will be published in open access journals and will be presented in
international conferences.

## **[3.2.1 Justification for the importance of the scientific problem and the requested resources (max 2000 characters)]{.mark}**

[By using high plex imaging at a subcellular resolution we can scan the
spatial expression of many relevant proteins in a tissue. In our study,
we are using tCycif highplex imaging in ovarian cancer tumoral samples.
In our current experiment, we have scanned images that allow us to spot

proteins in a tissue. In our study,
we are using tCycif highplex imaging in ovarian cancer tumoral samples.
In our current experiment, we have scanned images that allow us to spot
the expression of 34 different proteins representative of different cell
types and tumoral functions like angiogenesis, protein adhesion, and
immune evasion. By the detailed analysis of these images, we can
discover patterns associated with clinical prognosis or distinguish the
occurrence of certain immune evasion events. Our datasets also include
whole transcriptomic gene-expression matrices from matching tissue
sections/areas.]{.mark}

[Traditional approaches for the analysis of high plex images consist of
the detection/segmentation of cells and subsequently the extraction of
marker intensity per cell. We are working on developing a new approach
(TMEncoder) that directly analyses the images to train variational
autoencoder models to predict features associated with clinical
outcomes. This process is heavily computationally demanding. By the use
of this deep learning approach, we overcome the limitations of cell
segmentation and we can also focus on the diffusion of proteins through
the tumor microenvironment. We aim to extract features that can allow us
to identify the samples that potentially benefit from
immunotherapy.]{.mark}

3D, and many nuclei can be on different planes. Additionally, cells have

segmentation. However, tissues are
3D, and many nuclei can be on different planes. Additionally, cells have
different structures, with some being elongated and others more round,
which can affect the performance of cell segmentation approaches for
studying the tumor microenvironment. Because of this, we are
implementing deep learning approaches that use all the pixels of the
images in a cell segmentation manner.]{.mark}

[We are currently utilizing variational autoencoder architectures to
non-linearly reduce the images into latent spaces. Subsequently, we use
these latent spaces to train machine learning classifiers to identify
the image features (patches) that are most significantly associated with
clinical outcomes or immune evasion. The training process is
time-consuming, and different combinations of channels increase
computation times. We are in the phase of identifying the best
architectures for this analysis. The use of a high-performance computing
environment would enable us to test different architectures in parallel
and process the entire volume of data. Eventually, this pixel level
information can be integrated with spatial transcriptomics from the same
tissue regions to identify the molecular underpinnings of immune escape
in ovarian cancer.]{.mark}

[We will start our experiments with 176,000 CyCif images (patches
created from bigger slides). Those slide images are currently stored in
Allas CSC service. We will download all the slides from Allas into LUMI
and run the patch separation preprocessing script. We plan to expand
this dataset further. We expect to get as an output the model weights
(163 MB for the architectures we are currently exploring). We will store
the model weights for the current epoch and for the best-performing
epoch (based on performance metrics). We are mainly using these images
as input for our PyTorch models and have already optimized the

Data transfers are primarily batch-based and occur during dataset

**Transfer methods:**

- Secure transfer via CSC-supported tools (e.g., rsync, scp, or Allas

- Bulk transfers performed outside peak hours when possible

**Data management strategy:**

- Raw datasets transferred once and reused across jobs

- Derived data aggregated before download

- Temporary files cleaned regularly

- Preference for in-place analysis to reduce network load

## **[3.9.5 I/O]{.mark}**

[Please describe the I/O strategy of the code (for example usage of I/O
libraries, MPI I/O, netcdf, HDF5 or other approaches) (max 1000
characters):]{.mark}

[For image preprocessing, we use the Python multiprocessing library. As
for the model training and saving model weights, we will use the PyTorch
model save methods.]{.mark}

[The workflow relies primarily on Python-based I/O. Image preprocessing
is parallelized using the Python multiprocessing library, where workers
process independent files to minimize filesystem contention. Standard

Python-based I/O. Image preprocessing
is parallelized using the Python multiprocessing library, where workers
process independent files to minimize filesystem contention. Standard
Python I/O together with NumPy and Pandas loaders are used for tabular
and image data. During model training, intermediate data are retained in
memory when feasible to reduce disk pressure. Model checkpoints and
final weights are written using native PyTorch serialization
(torch.save). The workload is largely embarrassingly parallel and does
not require MPI I/O, NetCDF, or HDF5 at present. File access patterns
are mostly sequential and batched, and temporary files are periodically
cleaned to limit filesystem load.]{.mark}

**Description of files in this folder:**

- **Core_stats_Ncancer_cells.csv**: Number of cancer cells per core
 > and number of total cells per core. Only are listed all cores that
 > passed an internal QC, based on the number of cells per core, and
 > also the removal of cores with high noise (based on visual
 > inspection). In this file, only the cores with at least one cancer
 > cell.

- **Patients_survGroup.csv:** List of HRD and HRP samples and clinical
 > information by patient. The variables *refage*, *Figo_stage* and
 > *resdx* are covariables important associated with *timelastu*
 > (time of last follow up or total survival time of patient). The
 > column "HR.surv.category" was used before in Machine learning
 > models for class for prediction. Independently run models for PDS
 > or IDS indicated in "therapy_binary". The class "HR.surv.category"
 > was calculated using the variable *timelastfu*, dividing the
 > number of the patients in 3 equal size groups, independently for
 > HRD and HRP and PDS and IDS. Use the table
 > "Molecular_profile_cores.csv" to track the tCycif cores that
 > correspond to each patient.

 > number of the patients in 3 equal size groups, independently for
 > HRD and HRP and PDS and IDS. Use the table
 > "Molecular_profile_cores.csv" to track the tCycif cores that
 > correspond to each patient.

**Code exists in the repo:** https://github.com/farkkilab/pixel_ai

In Mahti, each person has their own folder in
/projappl/project_2003009/PERSON_NAME, for example
/projappl/project_2003009/matias/pixel_ai

It was organized like this because some of the libraries required local
installation, and the amount of space assigned to each person\'s home
folder was not enough

**Common data is located in /scratch/project_2003009**

Explanation of each folder in this location:

- Launonen_TMA: Cores used Launonen paper

- Launonen_TMA_patches: tiles created out of Launoen cores

- Launonen_histoprep_embeddings_gigapath: embeddings created out of
 > tiles from previous step, using gigapath

- Launonen_histoprep_embeddings_uni: same as previous step, but using
 > UNI model

- MHCII_histoprep_embeddings_gigapath: MHCII, provided by Ada.
 > Embeddings from those images using Gigapath

- MHCII_histoprep_embeddings_uni: Same as previous step, but using UNI
 > model

- NKI_histoprep_patches: Patches of NKI cores, created using logic
 > based on histoprep

- NKI_project_TMAs: Original images of NKI TMAs cores

- NKI_whole_slides_validation_MHCII: Original data from MHCII project

- NKI_wsi_MHCII_histoprep_patches: tiles created out of MHCII images,
 > using a logic based on histoprep

- PKG-PTR-HGSOC: TCGA data

- TCGA: TCGA download data

- He_space_slideflow: folder to stored Space SlideFlow project

- He_space_slideflow_cropped: folder to stored Space SlideFlow project
 > after cropping images

- Hf_cache: cache folder to store hugging face models (they are too
 > big to store in projappl, where we have a smaller storage space)

- Histoprep_embeddings_uni: tiles embedding using UNI model, from NKI
 > data

- Public_he: Data related to TCGA

- Slideflow_space_tcga: SlideFlow folder for TCGA

- Space_he: Space raw H&E images

- Transformers_cache: cache folder to store hugging face models (they
 > are too big to store in projappl, where we have a smaller storage
 > space)

# Pixel_AI brainstorming

## The idea of Pixel-based

- Each spot in a tissue could be considered as mixture of tissue
 > archetypes (for example tumor infiltration contains xx% of tumor
 > and xx% of immune cells). But we don't know the archetypes yet.

- Finding the archetypes means finding the niches in tissue - this
 > will be the new framework of describing and analyzing a tissue in
 > spatial molecular profiling.

- The idea is to infer the archetype/features from multi-channel
 > informations.

 - Consider PCA but in an explainable way. Reduce the 30+
 > dimensions (#channel) into \<10 (# tissue) dimensions.

- We could use deep learning models to solve our feature extraction
 > task. For example currently we are using VAE model. But is it the
 > best?

## How to evaluate the performance of our model

- Visualize our latent space

 - UMAP, current no colors

 - Color by labels. But what label do we have?

- Define downstream tasks

 - We have molecular profile labels for cores, maybe we could use
 > latent space to predict molecular profile.

 - 

## How to overcome multi-channel problem

Most CV models are designed to process RGB images (3 dimension), but we
have 20+ channels (20+ dimension)

- Fusion.

## How to investigate more on spatial information

- Grad-CAM:

Proposed approach

Divide each TIFF image input (33 channels) into small tiles (tested so 32x32x33, will test 128x128x33) 
Train a CNN VAE with those images (basic architecture) 
Extract latent space for each tile, using trained model 
Visualize latent space using T-SNE and UMAP 
Association of latent space (clustering, classifier?) with molecular/clinical traits of the cohort.

Proposed approach

Divide each TIFF image input (33 channels) into small tiles (tested so 32x32x33, will test 128x128x33) 
Train a CNN VAE with those images (basic architecture)

Association of latent space (clustering, classifier?) with molecular/clinical traits of the cohort.

# A deep learning framework for quantitative spatial analysis of the tumor microenvironment

**Ziqi Kang^1^, Matias Aiskovich^1^, Fernando Perez^1^, Angela Szabo^1^,
Lilian van Wagensveld^5,\ 6,\ 7^, Hugo Horlings^7^,** **Esa
Pitkänen^2,\ 3,\ 4^, Anniina Färkkilä^1,\ 2,\ 3^**

^1^ Research Program in Systems Oncology, University of Helsinki,
Finland

^2^ Institute for Molecular Medicine Finland, Helsinki Institute of Life
Sciences, University of Helsinki, Finland

^3^ iCAN Digital Precision Cancer Medicine Flagship, Finland

^6^ GROW, School for Oncology and Developmental Biology, Maastricht
University, 6229 ER Maastricht, the Netherlands

^7^ Division of Molecular Pathology, The Netherlands Cancer Institute,
1066 CX Amsterdam, The Netherlands

Current multiplexed immunofluorescence (mIF) image analysis relies on
quantitative description of single-cell objects, however, cell
segmentation is becoming a bottleneck for tissue architecture
description since it fails in dense regions and causes loss of
information in extracellular areas. There is a need to define novel
representations of complex tissue architecture, one possible solution is

vector_chunks.source_file: Abstract for EACR Cancer Multiomics and Computational Biology.md
vector_chunks.text:
ls in dense regions and causes loss of
information in extracellular areas. There is a need to define novel
representations of complex tissue architecture, one possible solution is
to extract biologically meaningful multi-scale features directly from
images without cell segmentation.

We present TMEncoder, a deep learning framework based on the variational
autoencoder (VAE) capable of extracting features of tumor
microenvironments based on multiplex-stained tumor tissue images without
expert-annotated labels. Based on the scale of input datasets, TMEncoder
could unsupervised learn corresponding features from local tissue
archetypes to tumor subtypes. TMEncoder incorporates multiple advanced
techniques including patch generation, intermediate fusion strategy, and
a novel pipeline for quantitative analysis of tumor microenvironment
elements. Therefore, our framework can process multi-channel images.

We applied TMEncoder to an in-house 33-plex tissue microarray (TMA)
dataset collected from a cohort of 241 high-grade serous ovarian cancer
(HGSOC) patients and successfully recovered biological features related
to tumor molecular subtypes. Then, we are planning to expand the
analysis to two external datasets of human cancer tissue images to
discover the tumor microenvironment features: a 56-plex TMA dataset
including 140 images from 35 advanced-stage colorectal cancer (CRC)

vector_chunks.text:
l datasets of human cancer tissue images to
discover the tumor microenvironment features: a 56-plex TMA dataset
including 140 images from 35 advanced-stage colorectal cancer (CRC)
patients; and a 35-plex imaging mass cytometry (IMC) dataset from 416
lung adenocarcinoma patients.

In summary, the novel image analysis strategy enabled by TMEncoder shows
promise to strengthen the understanding of tumor biology and re-visit
the hidden information behind multiplexed images. Combining such
information with classical single-cell-based analysis leads to an
improved understanding of the tumor microenvironment and novel precision
therapies.

## **2 SUMMARY OF THE PROJECT (PUBLIC ABSTRACT)**

*CSC will use this abstract (with some other basic information, such as
the size of the project) when publishing the selected proposals. **Max
1000 characters**.*

We aim to study the tumor microenvironment (TME) of high-grade serous
ovarian cancer to understand the basis of immune evasion and identify
cell-cell spatial biomarkers associated with clinical prognosis.

Using highly multiplexed imaging for single-cell spatial profiling, we
identified a distinct TME with evidence of increased spatial
immunosurveillance and distinct prognostication in ovarian cancer. In
this ERC Starting grant- funded project, we are employing highplex
microscopy and deep learning approach to identify characteristics in the
TME associated with the clinical outcomes in a large dataset of 300
patients participating in the Oncosys-OVA clinical trial (NCT06117384)
at the Helsinki university Hospital. Our current approach overcomes the
limitations of cell segmentation, as it uses patches of
highly-multiplexed images as input to identify common structures
associated with the clinico-molecular characteristics of the tumours.

## **3.1 KEY SCIENTIFIC/SOCIETAL/TECHNOLOGICAL CONTRIBUTION OF THE PROPOSAL (MAX 2000 CHARACTERS)**

[Outline the scientific/societal/technological importance of your

## **3.1 KEY SCIENTIFIC/SOCIETAL/TECHNOLOGICAL CONTRIBUTION OF THE PROPOSAL (MAX 2000 CHARACTERS)**

[Outline the scientific/societal/technological importance of your
project, how high-performance computing (HPC) will help you achieve your
goals and what the major expected outcomes are.]{.mark}

Every year 240 000 women are diagnosed, and 150 000 women die from
ovarian cancer (OC) worldwide. High-grade serous OC (HGSC) is the most
common and most lethal subtype of ovarian cancer. Around 50% of HGSC
patients are treated with DNA damaging chemotherapies in the neoadjuvant
setting. However, the majority of these patients develop
chemoresistance, and new treatments are urgently needed for HGSC. It has
been shown that some mutations of HGSC can shape its tumor
microenvironment in a way that patients could tentatively get benefits
from immunotherapy. Ovarian cancer response to immunotherapy is still
limited, and a better understanding of the tumor microenvironment (TME)
is required.

We are developing tools and a novel pipeline for the analysis of high
plex imaging combined with spatial transcriptomics of the TME. Our tools
are based on the use of GPUs for the deep learning analysis of a large
volume of imaging and transcriptomics data. The results of this project
will be published in open access journals and will be presented in
international conferences.

[By using high plex imaging at a subcellular resolution we can scan the
spatial expression of many relevant proteins in a tissue. In our study,
we are using tCycif highplex imaging in ovarian cancer tumoral samples.
In our current experiment, we have scanned images that allow us to spot

proteins in a tissue. In our study,
we are using tCycif highplex imaging in ovarian cancer tumoral samples.
In our current experiment, we have scanned images that allow us to spot
the expression of 34 different proteins representative of different cell
types and tumoral functions like angiogenesis, protein adhesion, and
immune evasion. By the detailed analysis of these images, we can
discover patterns associated with clinical prognosis or distinguish the
occurrence of certain immune evasion events. Our datasets also include
whole transcriptomic gene-expression matrices from matching tissue
sections/areas.]{.mark}

[Traditional approaches for the analysis of high plex images consist of
the detection/segmentation of cells and subsequently the extraction of
marker intensity per cell. We are working on developing a new approach
(TMEncoder) that directly analyses the images to train variational
autoencoder models to predict features associated with clinical
outcomes. This process is heavily computationally demanding. By the use
of this deep learning approach, we overcome the limitations of cell
segmentation and we can also focus on the diffusion of proteins through
the tumor microenvironment. We aim to extract features that can allow us
to identify the samples that potentially benefit from
immunotherapy.]{.mark}

segmentation. However, tissues are
3D, and many nuclei can be on different planes. Additionally, cells have
different structures, with some being elongated and others more round,
which can affect the performance of cell segmentation approaches for
studying the tumor microenvironment. Because of this, we are
implementing deep learning approaches that use all the pixels of the
images in a cell segmentation manner.]{.mark}

[We are currently utilizing variational autoencoder architectures to
non-linearly reduce the images into latent spaces. Subsequently, we use
these latent spaces to train machine learning classifiers to identify
the image features (patches) that are most significantly associated with
clinical outcomes or immune evasion. The training process is
time-consuming, and different combinations of channels increase
computation times. We are in the phase of identifying the best
architectures for this analysis. The use of a high-performance computing
environment would enable us to test different architectures in parallel
and process the entire volume of data. Eventually, this pixel level
information can be integrated with spatial transcriptomics from the same
tissue regions to identify the molecular underpinnings of immune escape
in ovarian cancer.]{.mark}

## **[3.9.5 I/O]{.mark}**

[Please describe the I/O strategy of the code (for example usage of I/O
libraries, MPI I/O, netcdf, HDF5 or other approaches) (max 1000

[The workflow relies primarily on Python-based I/O. Image preprocessing
is parallelized using the Python multiprocessing library, where workers
process independent files to minimize filesystem contention. Standard

Python-based I/O. Image preprocessing
is parallelized using the Python multiprocessing library, where workers
process independent files to minimize filesystem contention. Standard
Python I/O together with NumPy and Pandas loaders are used for tabular
and image data. During model training, intermediate data are retained in
memory when feasible to reduce disk pressure. Model checkpoints and
final weights are written using native PyTorch serialization
(torch.save). The workload is largely embarrassingly parallel and does
not require MPI I/O, NetCDF, or HDF5 at present. File access patterns
are mostly sequential and batched, and temporary files are periodically
cleaned to limit filesystem load.]{.mark}

LUMI-G, particularly porting CUDA-based models to the AMD GPU stack
(ROCm/HIP). This includes validating PyTorch ROCm compatibility,
optimizing data pipelines for GPU utilization, and benchmarking
multi-GPU scaling performance on LUMI.]{.mark}

**Description of files in this folder:**

- **Core_stats_Ncancer_cells.csv**: Number of cancer cells per core
 > and number of total cells per core. Only are listed all cores that
 > passed an internal QC, based on the number of cells per core, and
 > also the removal of cores with high noise (based on visual
 > inspection). In this file, only the cores with at least one cancer
 > cell.

- **Patients_survGroup.csv:** List of HRD and HRP samples and clinical
 > information by patient. The variables *refage*, *Figo_stage* and
 > *resdx* are covariables important associated with *timelastu*
 > (time of last follow up or total survival time of patient). The
 > column "HR.surv.category" was used before in Machine learning
 > models for class for prediction. Independently run models for PDS
 > or IDS indicated in "therapy_binary". The class "HR.surv.category"
 > was calculated using the variable *timelastfu*, dividing the
 > number of the patients in 3 equal size groups, independently for
 > HRD and HRP and PDS and IDS. Use the table
 > "Molecular_profile_cores.csv" to track the tCycif cores that
 > correspond to each patient.

# Meeting 27.02.2024

\- Esa suggested using the multi-channel of the whole image

\- The reconstruction was not very sharp. What is the latest dimension?
Is it possible to improve the latent size to 15?

\- Is the title enough to be classified?

\- Are there batch effects?

\- Take all the features of the tiles. Design pooling strategies.

\- Esa suggests randomly sampling the same amount of tiles per image.

\- Anniina suggests predicting the therapy sequence as a downstream
task.

\- Esa suggests concatenating the features from each VAE, as a matrix.
Every row for each tile.

\- Essa suggests plotting ROC, and AUC curves for the classification.

\- For the classification, to make the classification balanced, Esa

# Pixel_AI brainstorming

## The idea of Pixel-based

- Each spot in a tissue could be considered as mixture of tissue
 > archetypes (for example tumor infiltration contains xx% of tumor
 > and xx% of immune cells). But we don't know the archetypes yet.

- Finding the archetypes means finding the niches in tissue - this
 > will be the new framework of describing and analyzing a tissue in
 > spatial molecular profiling.

- The idea is to infer the archetype/features from multi-channel
 > informations.

 - Consider PCA but in an explainable way. Reduce the 30+
 > dimensions (#channel) into \<10 (# tissue) dimensions.

- We could use deep learning models to solve our feature extraction
 > task. For example currently we are using VAE model. But is it the
 > best?

## How to evaluate the performance of our model

- Visualize our latent space

 - UMAP, current no colors

 - Color by labels. But what label do we have?

- Define downstream tasks

 - We have molecular profile labels for cores, maybe we could use
 > latent space to predict molecular profile.

 - 

## How to overcome multi-channel problem

Most CV models are designed to process RGB images (3 dimension), but we
have 20+ channels (20+ dimension)

- Fusion.

## How to investigate more on spatial information

- Grad-CAM:

**Code exists in the repo:** https://github.com/farkkilab/pixel_ai

In Mahti, each person has their own folder in
/projappl/project_2003009/PERSON_NAME, for example
/projappl/project_2003009/matias/pixel_ai

It was organized like this because some of the libraries required local
installation, and the amount of space assigned to each person\'s home
folder was not enough

**Common data is located in /scratch/project_2003009**

Explanation of each folder in this location:

- Launonen_TMA: Cores used Launonen paper

- Launonen_TMA_patches: tiles created out of Launoen cores

- Launonen_histoprep_embeddings_gigapath: embeddings created out of
 > tiles from previous step, using gigapath

- Launonen_histoprep_embeddings_uni: same as previous step, but using
 > UNI model

- MHCII_histoprep_embeddings_gigapath: MHCII, provided by Ada.
 > Embeddings from those images using Gigapath

- MHCII_histoprep_embeddings_uni: Same as previous step, but using UNI
 > model

- NKI_histoprep_patches: Patches of NKI cores, created using logic
 > based on histoprep

- NKI_project_TMAs: Original images of NKI TMAs cores

- NKI_whole_slides_validation_MHCII: Original data from MHCII project

- NKI_wsi_MHCII_histoprep_patches: tiles created out of MHCII images,
 > using a logic based on histoprep

- PKG-PTR-HGSOC: TCGA data

- TCGA: TCGA download data

Proposed approach

Divide each TIFF image input (33 channels) into small tiles (tested so 32x32x33, will test 128x128x33) 
Train a CNN VAE with those images (basic architecture) 
Extract latent space for each tile, using trained model 
Visualize latent space using T-SNE and UMAP 
Association of latent space (clustering, classifier?) with molecular/clinical traits of the cohort.

Proposed approach

Extract latent space for each tile, using trained model

Association of latent space (clustering, classifier?) with molecular/clinical traits of the cohort.
